notable.notable_blurb: Connecticut Lieutenant Governor 1816-1823, Superior Court Judge

cross_connections.display_label: Both buried Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven — Hooker descendants

narrative_blocks.header: Lieutenant Governor of Connecticut, 1816-1823
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan Ingersoll Jr. served as Lieutenant Governor of Connecticut from 1816 until his death in 1823 — the role making him a principal political figure of the early-Republic era in the state. The Lt Governor's office in Connecticut was a significant constitutional post during the years following the 1818 Constitution. His son Ralph I. Ingersoll later described him as 'a gentleman of great moral worth, and among the foremost in his profession.'

narrative_blocks.header: State Attorney, Superior Court Judge, then Lt Governor
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan rose through three principal Connecticut public-service offices in succession — first State Attorney, then a Judge of the Superior Court, then Lieutenant Governor. The progression took him from prosecutorial to judicial to political leadership across the late-eighteenth and early-nineteenth centuries. His judicial reputation rested on the same qualities of moral worth and professional standing that distinguished his political tenure.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of US Minister to Russia Ralph Ingersoll
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan's son Ralph I. Ingersoll (born February 8, 1789) followed him into the law and the public service — eight years in the United States Congress (1825-1833), State Attorney for twelve years, Connecticut Minister Plenipotentiary to the Russian court 1846-1848 under President Polk. The Ingersoll legal dynasty was one of the longest-running Connecticut family practices of the nineteenth century. A grandson Charles Roberts Ingersoll became Governor of Connecticut 1873-1877.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

Ingersoll later described him as 'a gentleman of great moral worth, and among the foremost in his profession.'

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

notable.notable_blurb: US Minister to Russia under Polk; Congressman 1825-33

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT, 3 Maple Ave East

narrative_blocks.header: US Minister to Russia under President Polk
narrative_blocks.body: Ralph Isaacs Ingersoll was appointed by Democratic President James K. Polk as the sixteenth United States Minister Plenipotentiary to the Russian Empire on August 8, 1846. He presented his credentials in Russia on May 30, 1847, and served until his resignation on July 1, 1848. The appointment was unsought and unexpected — he served his government 'faithfully and ably' before gladly returning to his New Haven law practice.

narrative_blocks.header: Four consecutive Congressional terms 1825-1833
narrative_blocks.body: Ralph served four consecutive terms in the United States Congress representing Connecticut at-large from March 4, 1825 through March 3, 1833 — first elected as an Adams candidate, then reelected as Anti-Jacksonian. He served on the powerful Ways and Means committee for four years, holding the second-ranking position during his final term. He was not a candidate for renomination in 1832.

narrative_blocks.header: Declined US Senate seat from Governor Edwards, 1835
narrative_blocks.body: In 1835 Governor Henry W. Edwards offered Ralph the United States Senate seat made vacant by the death of Senator Nathan Smith. He peremptorily declined — though the legislature would undoubtedly have elected him to the position. He similarly declined repeated gubernatorial nominations from his party across many years, departing from his settled purpose only once when Polk appointed him to Russia.

narrative_blocks.header: Best public speaker his contemporaries ever knew
narrative_blocks.body: One of Ralph's contemporaries with wide experience said of him 'he was the best public speaker I ever knew.' His voice was pleasant and almost musical, with unusual compass — distinct in its lowest tones. He was eminently persuasive before a jury or popular assembly, with a documented personal magnetism that allowed him to sway crowds, by turns humorous and pathetic, sarcastic when needed.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1808, New Haven law, Grove Street burial
narrative_blocks.body: Ralph graduated from Yale College in 1808 and read law for two years with Seth P. Staples before opening his New Haven practice. He married Margaret Catherine Eleanor Van den Heuvel in 1814 and they had seven children together — including future Governor of Connecticut Charles Roberts Ingersoll and US Congressman Colin Macrae Ingersoll. He died in New Haven August 26, 1872 at age eighty-three, buried at Grove Street Cemetery.

institutions.notes: Studied at Yale Law School after Yale College 1808

parents.mother_research_notes: Daughter of NY landowner Charles Ward Apthorp

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT
Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law; US Rep NY

narrative_blocks.header: US Congressman from Connecticut, 1851-1855
narrative_blocks.body: Colin Macrae Ingersoll served two terms as a United States Congressman from Connecticut's 2nd district from March 4, 1851 through March 3, 1855 — succeeding Walter Booth, succeeded by John Woodruff. He continued the Ingersoll family's third generation in Congress alongside his father Ralph Isaacs Ingersoll (CT Congressman 1825-1833) and his cousin Charles Jared Ingersoll of Philadelphia. Connecticut had three concurrent Ingersolls in mid-nineteenth-century politics including his brother Charles R. Ingersoll as Governor.

narrative_blocks.header: Secretary of legation St Petersburg during father's ministry
narrative_blocks.body: Colin served as Secretary of the United States legation at St. Petersburg Russia 1847-1848 — during his father Ralph Isaacs Ingersoll's term as US Minister to Russia under President Polk. In 1848 he served as acting Chargé d'Affaires at the St Petersburg post. The Ingersoll father-son diplomatic Russia posting was a rare American family pattern, taking both generations into the Tsarist court.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Julia Harriet Pratt, daughter of Zadock Pratt
narrative_blocks.body: Colin married Julia Harriet Pratt in 1853 — daughter of US Representative Zadock Pratt of New York, the tanner-banker who built the world's largest tannery at Prattsville NY in 1824. The marriage united two American congressional families. Their four children included future US Minister to Siam George Pratt Ingersoll (1861-1927).

narrative_blocks.header: Died age eighty-four pneumonia, same year as brother
narrative_blocks.body: Colin Macrae Ingersoll died September 13, 1903 in New Haven Connecticut at age eighty-four — the same year his brother Charles Roberts Ingersoll (Governor of Connecticut 1873-1877) also died in January 1903. The two Ingersoll brothers were both interred at Grove Street Cemetery New Haven. The cause of death was pneumonia.

institutions.notes: Yale College + Yale Law School graduate 1839
Trinity College 1839; founding member Phi Kappa Society

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

cross_connections.display_label: New Haven attorney; Bristol law firm founder, almost lifelong friend
cross_connections.notes: Lifelong friendship documented in Bristol's CT Reports memorial. Bristol senior counsel for Yale University, founder of Bristol Stoddard & Bristol 1888.

cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law; US Navy Rear Admiral

narrative_blocks.header: Governor of Connecticut, 1873-1877
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Roberts Ingersoll served as Governor of Connecticut from 1873 through 1877 — four years that placed him at the head of the state during Reconstruction-era industrial expansion. He continued the family's prominent role in Connecticut public life that his father Ralph (US Minister to Russia, four-term Congressman) and grandfather Jonathan Ingersoll Jr. (Lieutenant Governor 1816-1823) had established before him. Three consecutive generations of Ingersolls held principal Connecticut state offices.

narrative_blocks.header: Almost lifelong friendship with Louis H. Bristol
narrative_blocks.body: Charles maintained an unusually close, almost lifelong friendship with New Haven attorney Louis H. Bristol — the senior counsel for Yale University and founder of the Bristol Stoddard Bristol law firm. The friendship was described as 'one of special closeness' that ran from their early careers at the New Haven bar through to Bristol's 1910 death. Both men were New Haven figures of the same generation, both descendants of prominent Connecticut legal families.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Admiral Francis Gregory's daughter Virginia
narrative_blocks.body: Charles married Virginia Gregory (1823-1898), daughter of Admiral Francis Gregory of the United States Navy. The couple had five children together — only three of whom survived to adulthood, two infant deaths and a childhood death marking the family. Virginia died November 30, 1898 in New Haven at age seventy-five, five years before Charles followed in 1903.

narrative_blocks.header: North Haven 1821 to 1903, age eighty-one
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Roberts Ingersoll was born September 16, 1821 in North Haven Connecticut and died January 25, 1903 in North Haven at age eighty-one. He is buried at Grove Street Cemetery in New Haven alongside three generations of the Ingersoll family. The Grove Street cluster includes his father Ralph, grandfather Jonathan Jr., his wife Virginia Gregory, and his children.

institutions.notes: Yale College class of 1840; honorary LLD 1874

notable.notable_blurb: Judge, US District Court for the District of Connecticut

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

narrative_blocks.header: US federal judge nominated by President Pierce
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Anthony Ingersoll served as a United States federal judge, nominated to the bench by President Franklin Pierce in the 1850s. The appointment placed him on the Connecticut federal bench during the politically charged decade leading into the Civil War. He continued the Ingersoll family's principal role in Connecticut legal and judicial life — his father Jonathan Jr. having served as Superior Court Judge and Lieutenant Governor, his elder brother Ralph in Congress and as US Minister to Russia.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of two sons who continued legal tradition
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Anthony's two sons Charles Dennis Ingersoll (1843-1905, New York City lawyer) and Thomas Chester Ingersoll (1845-1884) continued the family legal tradition. The Ingersoll name extended across three generations of the New York and Philadelphia bars in addition to the original Connecticut branch. His brother Charles Jared Ingersoll (the Philadelphia cousin) had been the family progenitor of the Pennsylvania legal line.

narrative_blocks.header: Born 1798, died 1860 age sixty-two
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Anthony Ingersoll was born in 1798 — likely a twin to his sister Harriet Ingersoll Voorhees who shares the same birth year. He died in 1860 at age sixty-two on the eve of the Civil War. His father Jonathan Ingersoll Jr. had been Lieutenant Governor of Connecticut at the time of his birth and continued in that role until his death in 1823.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT
Green-Wood Cemetery, Greenwood Heights Brooklyn NY

narrative_blocks.header: NYC judge continuing Ingersoll legal tradition
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Dennis Ingersoll continued the Ingersoll family legal tradition across the New York and Connecticut bars — making him the fourth consecutive generation of Ingersoll lawyers and judges descending from Rev. Jonathan Ingersoll. His grandfather Jonathan Ingersoll Jr. was Lieutenant Governor of Connecticut; his father Charles Anthony Ingersoll was a US federal judge; his uncle Ralph Isaacs Ingersoll was a US Congressman and Minister to Russia; his cousin Charles Roberts Ingersoll became Governor of Connecticut. The Ingersoll legal dynasty spanned 1747-1905 (Rev. Jonathan to Charles Dennis) across five generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Married widow Katharine Sanders Varnum, 1885
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Dennis married Katharine Corse Sanders Varnum on June 2, 1885 in Manhattan New York — she had previously been married to Robert Taylor Varnum. The couple had three children together: Hamilton Ingersoll (1888-1940), Anita Ingersoll Gwynne (1891-1970), and Justine Ingersoll Arnold (1893-1984). His grandchildren through these three children form the documented twentieth-century Ingersoll-Arnold-Gwynne descendants of the original Connecticut bar family.

narrative_blocks.header: New Haven 1843 to Manhattan 1905, Green-Wood Cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Dennis Ingersoll was born January 15, 1843 in New Haven Connecticut and died January 8, 1905 in Manhattan New York at age sixty-one. He is buried at Green-Wood Cemetery in Greenwood Heights Brooklyn New York — breaking the Ingersoll family Grove Street Cemetery tradition through his NYC career. His wife Katharine died in Menton France in 1922.

burial.plot_notes: Chapel of the Pines Crematory, Los Angeles CA

narrative_blocks.header: Tapped for Wolf’s Head at Yale
narrative_blocks.body: At Yale he was elected to Wolf’s Head, one of the secret senior societies, among a crowd of club memberships. A single year at Albany Law School followed, and he was practicing in New Haven by 1869.

narrative_blocks.header: The court clerk died in Los Angeles
narrative_blocks.body: Son of a federal judge and brother to New York and Connecticut lawyers, the Superior Court clerk spent his career in New Haven’s courthouse — yet died in Los Angeles in 1926 and was cremated at the Chapel of the Pines.

military_service.unit: US sloop-of-war Preble, Mediterranean squadron under Commodore Morgan
military_service.notes: Commander of the US sloop-of-war Preble in the Mediterranean squadron. Died at Smyrna Jul 27, 1842 of bilious fever. Per official Navy report: 'an officer of a high order of merit, and his death is a serious loss to his country.'

narrative_blocks.body: In 1777, freed his enslaved man Cyphax (age 20) with selectmen approval — one of few documented manumissions in Ridgefield

battles: Amherst's campaign 1759 — Fort Carillon to Oswego to St. Lawrence
military_service.notes: Chaplain in Abercromby's ill-fated attack on Fort Carillon 1758. Wrote to a colleague that God showed "distinguishing mercy to the Connecticut Troops" who suffered few deaths in that dreadful slaughter. Following year served under Amherst from Fort Carillon to Oswego and down the St. Lawrence.

parents.father_research_notes: John Ingersoll (ca.1615–Sep 3, 1684) of Westfield, MA. Descended from John Ingersoll Sr. (1626–1684), born Edworth, Bedfordshire, England.
